coredata.dc:title: A facile synthesis of self-doped carbon dots from 2-aminoterephthalic acid and their applications
coredata.dc:creator: Tu, Yufei
Li, Guoqiang
Wang, Xuan
Wang, Shiqi
Cheng, Shijie
Zuo, Danying
Xu, Jing
Zhang, Hongwei
coredata.dc:description: Self-doped carbon dots with N elements (N-CDs) are successfully prepared through one-pot hydrothermal carbonization strategy by using 2-aminoterephthalic acid as a new precursor. The as-prepared N-CDs show a size of 5–10 nm and display wavelength-independent fluorescence. Their maximum excitation and emission wavelength are 340 and 423 nm, respectively. They demonstrate a highly selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+ ions in the range of 5–100 μM via an effective fluorescence quenching mechanism. Furthermore, the cotton fabrics treated by these N-CDs have excellent ultraviolet (UV)-protective property. Therefore, they have potential to be used for the detection of Fe3+ ions, the UV fluorescent ink and the UV protection for cotton fabrics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: 2-Aminoterephthalic acid
Hydrothermal carbonization
Carbon dot
Fluorescence
UV protection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity of Fe3+ detection

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic synthesis process of N-CDs via hydrothermal carbonization reaction of 2-aminoterephthalic acid.
(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of all sample solutions, (b) FT-IR spectra of ATA, CQD102 and CQD106, (c) TEM image of CQD102.
Possible reaction scheme of ATA toward the formation of N-CDs and the schematic for fluorescence quenching mechanism by Fe3+ ions.

Fluorescence spectra of the (a) CQD102, (b) CQD106 aqueous solution excited by different wavelengths ranging from 300 to 400 nm in 10 increments (Inset: optical images of CQD102 and CQD106 aqueous solutions under 365 nm UV light), (c) Fluorescence excitation spectra for 423 nm emission wavelength of all sample aqueous solutions, (d) Comparison of photoluminescence emission intensities at 423 nm.

Fluorescence changes of (a) CQD102 and (b) CQD106 in the presence of different metal ions, F0 and F stand for the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs before and after the addition of metal ions. The relationships between F/F0 and Fe3+ concentration of (c) CQD102 and (d) CQD106.
UV blocking properties of cotton fabrics treated by N-CQD solutions.
Comparison about detection limits of various CDs.

head.ce:title: A facile synthesis of self-doped carbon dots from 2-aminoterephthalic acid and their applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Wuhan Textile University, WuHan, 430073, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Wuhan Textile University, WuHan, 430073, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The two authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Self-doped carbon dots with N elements (N-CDs) are successfully prepared through one-pot hydrothermal carbonization strategy by using 2-aminoterephthalic acid as a new precursor. The as-prepared N-CDs show a size of 5–10 nm and display wavelength-independent fluorescence. Their maximum excitation and emission wavelength are 340 and 423 nm, respectively. They demonstrate a highly selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+ ions in the range of 5–100 μM via an effective fluorescence quenching mechanism. Furthermore, the cotton fabrics treated by these N-CDs have excellent ultraviolet (UV)-protective property. Therefore, they have potential to be used for the detection of Fe3+ ions, the UV fluorescent ink and the UV protection for cotton fabrics.

ce:section.ce:para: As a kind of zero-dimensional carbonic nanomaterials, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have inspired extensive attentions because of their several advantages over conventional metal quantum dots, such as low toxicity, good biocompatibility, low cost, superior chemical stability, high water solubility, environmentally-friendly preparation and easy functionalization [1,2]. They demonstrate great potential in numerous applications, including sensor, ion detection, photo-catalysis, bio-imaging, surface-enhanced Raman scattering, optoelectronics, energy storage and conversion [2,3]. In the past decade, two synthetic strategies have been widely adopted to prepare CDs [2,4]. One is the “top-down” route. The other is the “bottom-up” route. The former strategy employs bulky carbon structures as original materials, such as activated carbon [5], petroleum coke [6], graphite [7], candle soot [8]. On the other hand, the latter strategy involves small molecules as precursors, like ascorbic acid [9], citric acid [10], starch [11], gelatin [12,13], chitosan [14], sucrose [15], grass [16], phenylenediamine [17], saccharide [18], polyacrylamide [19], caffeine [20].

Cao et al. employed citric acid monohydrate and urea as the precursors to prepare fluorescent carbon nitride quantum dots (CNQD) by a microwave-assisted solvothermal method [10]. The CNQDs showed two maximum emission peaks at 450 and 540 nm and could be used to probe Hg2+ ions. Liu et al. used citric acid and glycine as cheap resources to manufacture CDs with blue fluorescence emission, which could label bovine serum albumin [21]. Guo et al. prepared N, P-codoped CDs through the thermal treatment of the mixture of citric acid and o-phosphorylethanolamine [22]. They emitted strong blue-green light under irradiation of 365 nm light and could enhance the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 for the degradation of dyes. Jiang et al. reported N-doped CDs with bright-yellow fluorescence emission by using 1,2,4-triaminobenzene as carbon precursors, which could be used for cellular imaging and detection of cysteine [23]. Chen et al. synthesized red-emissive CDs by hydrothermal treatment of p-phenylenediamine and phosphorus acid, which could be used for fingerprints detection [24]. Jiang et al. fabricated three CDs by heat o-, m- or p-phenylenediamine in ethanol solution, respectively, which could emit bright and stable red, green and blue colors of luminescence [17]. It suggested that the molecular structure of precursors had important effect on the fluorescence emission of CDs. Ding et al. hydrothermally synthesized CDs and separated them via silica column chromatography. The separated CDs emitted bright and stable luminescence in gradient colors from blue to red under 365 nm UV light [25]. They proved that different surface states of the CD samples resulted from different degrees of oxidation on their surfaces, ultimately determining the fluorescence colors.

However, although such remarkable progresses of preparing CDs have been achieved, new large-scale, inexpensive and green synthetic approaches of CDs still need to be developed. The 2-aminoterephthalic acid (ATA) has intense blue-violet fluorescence and serves as organic building block for construction of extended open framework [26,27]. Herein, we use ATA as the precursors to fabricate nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) via a facile and one-pot hydrothermal carbonization method. The synthetic process is illustrated in Fig. 1 . The as-fabricated N-CDs are utilized for the ultra-violet (UV) fluorescent ink, the detection of Fe3+ ions and the UV protection of cotton fabrics.

The N-CDs were synthesized through a one-pot hydrothermal carbonization method. In a typical synthetic procedure, 0.4 g ATA and 0.2 g NaOH were dissolved in 50 mL water at 60 °C under stirring. Subsequently, the solution was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and maintained at 160 °C for 12 h in an oven. After the autoclave was automatically cooled down, the resulting light-yellow solution was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (1000 Da) for 5 days to remove NaOH and unreacted ATA by refreshing the outside water of the bag periodically. Finally, the obtained N-CD solution in the dialysis bag was collected for further experiments. Reaction conditions of N-CDs are listed in Table 1 .

A field emission gun transmission electron microscope (FETEM, Tecnai G2 F30) was used to characterize the morphology and size of N-CDs. A FTIR spectrometer (VERTEX70) was adopted to record Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of N-CDs. An ultraviolet visible (UV–vis) spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan) was employed to measure UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CDs. A fluorescence spectrophotometer (RF-6000, Shimadzu, Japan) was utilized to test excitation and emission spectra of N-CDs. A Textile UV prevention instrument (UV-912E, China) was used to test ultraviolet protection factor (UPF) of cotton fabrics treated by N-CDs.

For a typical test, 5.0 μL of N-CDs suspension (5.0 mg mL−1) was firstly diluted with 3.0 mL of water. Secondly, pH value of the solution was adjusted to 2. Thirdly, different concentrations of Fe3+ was added in and mixed for 20 min. Finally, the fluorescence quenching spectra with the excitation wavelength of 340 nm was recorded.

Fig. 2 a is the UV–vis absorption spectra of ATA and N-CD aqueous solutions. The ATA aqueous solution demonstrates two typical absorption bands at 282 and 370 nm, whereas N-CDs have three evident absorption bands at 327, ˜260 and 224–237 nm, which is assigned to the electron transitions from the π (or n) orbital to the π* orbital of the CO, CN and CC bonds, respectively [28–31].

Fig. 2b displays the FT-IR spectra of ATA, CQD102 and CQD106. For the FT-IR spectrum of ATA, the absorption bands centered at 3508, 3392 and 1238 cm−1 can be attributed to the asymmetric NH2 stretching vibration, symmetric NH2 stretching vibration and C-NH2 stretching vibration, respectively. The peaks centered at 1690 cm−1 contributes with the CO stretching vibration. The peaks located at 1496 and 755 cm−1 are due to carbon-carbon stretching vibrations and ring breathing mode of benzene ring [26,27]. For the FT-IR spectra of CQD102 and CQD106, they have similar absorption bands except for the band at 1626 cm−1 which is assigned to aromatic CN stretching vibration in CQD106 due to the addition of ethylenediamine [10]. The broad peak centered at 3429 cm−1 is assigned to the OH and NH stretching vibrations. The absorption bands at 1250 and 1418 cm−1 are ascribed to the CO stretching vibration and OH in-plane deformation vibration. These results indicate the existence of abundant carboxylic acid and other oxygen-containing functional groups on the surfaces of CQD102 and CQD106 [10,13].

The FETEM image of CQD102 (Fig. 2c) reveals that CQD102 samples are approximately ellipsoidal morphology with particle sizes in the range from 5 to 10 nm. Furthermore, they demonstrate an obvious crystalline structure with a lattice parameter of 0.22 nm which is consistent with to the (100) in-plane lattice of graphene [25,32]. Based on the above analyses of experimental data, a possible reaction mechanism of ATA toward the formation of N-CDs is illustrated in Fig. 3 .

From photoluminescence (PL) spectra in Fig. 4 a, b and Fig. S1, it can be found that the typical spectrum comprises one emission peak centered at about 423 nm. All of the samples remain nearly unchanged emission peak positions when the excitation wavelength increases, which suggests that the fluorescence mechanism is based on the surface defect states rather than size-dependent interband transitions [2,15]. Fig. 4c shows the PL excitation (PLE) spectra for 423 nm of samples. The N-CDs display similar excitation peaks centered at about 343 nm, but the ATA exhibits an excitation peak at 374 nm. The distinct luminescence properties might be attributed to different sizes (Fig. S2, ATA diffuses out of the dialysis bag after 5 days) and different surface structures between N-CDs and ATA. The PL intensities of N-CDs at 423 nm are demonstrated in Fig. 4d. It can be observed that the CQD102 and CQD106 have stronger blue emission behaviors than the other samples. Furthermore, additional nitrogen sources don’t endow CQD104, CQD105 and CQD106 with significant advantages over the self-doped CQD102. As a consequence, the CQD102 is used as fluorescence ink to write three capital letters (WTU, the abbreviation for Wuhan Textile University) on a filter paper, which can maintain the original fluorescence intensity even after three months (Fig. S3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity of Fe3+ detection

ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that the UV radiation can easily transmit through ordinary cotton fabrics, which results in low UPF value (<15) [37]. Generally, the UPF ratings of less than 15, between 15 and 50, and more than 50 (50+) are classified as bad, good, and excellent UV blocking properties for textiles, respectively [38,39]. Consequently, it is important to develop cotton fabrics with good UV protection property for outdoor activities. After cotton fabrics are immersed in N-CD solutions and dried at 100 °C, their UPF values are measured and presented in Fig. 6 . Compared with the untreated cotton fabric, these cotton fabrics treated by N-CDs exhibit UPF values higher than 28. Among them, the cotton fabric treated by CQD102 has a UPF value of 32.4 and emits bright blue light under 365 nm UV light (Fig. S4), indicating good UV-blocking property. But they have a poor washing durability due to the soluble property of N-CDs, thereby a protective coating (e.g. polydimethylsiloxane) for N-CDs on cotton fabrics are needed in future further study.

In summary, a series of N-CDs are prepared from a new precursor by a facile one-pot hydrothermal method. These N-CDs demonstrate typical excitation-independent property and emit blue luminescence at the excitation wavelength of 340 nm. They also exhibit highly efficient and sensitive response to Fe3+ concentration and good UV protection for cotton fabrics. It is believed that as-prepared N-CDs can provide promising applications in information encryption, fluorescent probe, chemical sensor and UV protection.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declared that they have no conflicts of interest to this work.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Hubei Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (2018CFB267).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mtcomm.2019.100599.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
